title: Cirrus: add podman_machine_aarch64
body:
Depends on https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/14801

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
None
```

comments.body:
Task map is depressing: ![cirrus-map-pr15031](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/265639/180307010-756f01a4-9219-4152-bb51-0e071c73a599.png)

I'm not seeing any way that the separate arch64 tasks can be combined into the existing ones, so I suppose we have to live with it.

I'm seeing this as an attempt to get CI working, and am assuming that everyone's goal is to merge #14972 first, then #14719, then rebase and review this one. If that is not a correct assumption, please respond here.

> I'm seeing this as an attempt to get CI working, and am assuming that everyone's goal is to merge #14972 first, then #14719, then rebase and review this one. If that is not a correct assumption, please respond here.

@edsantiago Exactly as you said. I only included those cause @cevich said it contains some fixes.
@cevich remind me please, do i need some label to get the machine tests to run ?
can't quite figure out the exact failure in x86_64 machine test
@cevich @edsantiago would `test_podman_machine` also trigger aarch64 tests or does it need a new label? I don't see anything in the repo nor in the label info

As best I can tell, the new stanza is identical to the old one except for the AARCH64 parts (and whitespace differences). The tag should apply, I don't see anything in [cirrus docs](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/writing-tasks/#required-pr-labels) indicating that "it only works for one task" or anything like that. So, no idea why the new one didn't trigger, and no idea why the first one failed. Sorry.

> Task map is depressing:

Don't worry, it's only going to get worse :(  I think one issue is that we have completely separate aarch64 tasks instead of having them as part of a matrix.  The only way to realize that is to include the `gce_instance` and `ec2_instance` blocks under each of the various the matrix items.  So it's possible, but may be complex/ugly - we'd have to try it and see (probably in a separate PR).
Looking good @lsm5 seems like you're almost there.
piggybacks on https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/15244
@baude @cevich @edsantiago good to go :rocket:
Presumably the new tasks are failing due to it downloading the x86_64 FCOS image.  I think I heard someone is working on that?
I also see `ssh something` errors. `podman-machine` people PTAL.

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich*, *edsantiago*, *lsm5* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago,lsm5] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Failing ([x86](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5636711613988864/html/machine-podman-fedora-36-rootless-host.log.html#t--Basic-ops--1) and [aarch64](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5073761660567552/html/machine-podman-fedora-36-aarch64-rootless-host.log.html#t--Basic-ops--1)) in `Basic ops` with
```
Error: error creating host key callback function for /tmp/podman_test3529809326/.ssh/known_hosts: open /tmp/podman_test3529809326/.ssh/known_hosts: no such file or directory
```
podman-machine peeps, this is on you

> Failing ([x86](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5636711613988864/html/machine-podman-fedora-36-rootless-host.log.html#t--Basic-ops--1) and [aarch64](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5073761660567552/html/machine-podman-fedora-36-aarch64-rootless-host.log.html#t--Basic-ops--1)) in `Basic ops` with
> 
> ```
> Error: error creating host key callback function for /tmp/podman_test3529809326/.ssh/known_hosts: open /tmp/podman_test3529809326/.ssh/known_hosts: no such file or directory
> ```
> 
> podman-machine peeps, this is on you

pinged @baude on matrix.

> Failing ([x86](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5636711613988864/html/machine-podman-fedora-36-rootless-host.log.html#t--Basic-ops--1) and [aarch64](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5073761660567552/html/machine-podman-fedora-36-aarch64-rootless-host.log.html#t--Basic-ops--1)) in `Basic ops` with
> 
> ```
> Error: error creating host key callback function for /tmp/podman_test3529809326/.ssh/known_hosts: open /tmp/podman_test3529809326/.ssh/known_hosts: no such file or directory
> ```

I guess that’s introduced by https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/15094 , see the https://github.com/containers/common/pull/1094 for much more conversation about `known_hosts`. 

IIRC, basically, if `.ssh/known_hosts` doesn’t exist or doesn’t already contain an entry for the host, connection just fails.
The default to should to succeed, since doesn't it succeed if there are no known hosts in a file, or is this supposed to prompt the user?
In case it matters: Changes LGTM
Still failing with ssh error. Was there any reason to believe the error would be gone?

> Still failing with ssh error. Was there any reason to believe the error would be gone?

Everything passed on a manual run on aarch64 bare metal.
im able to replicate the failure locally now.

> The default to should to succeed, since doesn't it succeed if there are no known hosts in a file, or is this supposed to prompt the user?

Prompting would make sense but doesn’t currently exist AFAIK.

I don’t think just connecting with no known_hosts or any other way to authenticate the key is really an option, in general. `podman machine` might be special to the extent that a VM that was created by it is under much more control than any other remote host — but that should also make it fairly easy to add a `known_hosts` entry instead of designing a reliable special-case path.

> make it fairly easy to add a known_hosts entry

I was going to suggest we use something like `ssh-keyscan` to add them automatically.  However, since the host keys will change with every new VM, we would be filling the poor user's `known_hosts` forever with useless keys.  The intended `podman-machine` use-case strikes me as the reason `ssh` supports `-o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -o CheckHostIP=no` (the "I don't care" option).  So maybe `podman connection ...` commands need to support an `--lazy` mode which we enable by default for any `podman machine` connections?

> > make it fairly easy to add a known_hosts entry
> 
> I was going to suggest we use something like `ssh-keyscan` to add them automatically.

I was primarily thinking we could generate keys and provision them into the machine.

> However, since the host keys will change with every new VM, we would be filling the poor user's `known_hosts` forever with useless keys.

Creating and managing a special-purpose `known_hosts` for that single machine, and automatically that file for connecting to the machine, might solve that. Yes, that’s even more code to write…

---

> The intended `podman-machine` use-case strikes me as the reason `ssh` supports `-o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -o CheckHostIP=no` (the "I don't care" option). 

That might ultimately be the right call, I don’t know enough about the situation. For me, the decisive question is whether we are certain enough about the created networking setup to be _sure_ that we are talking to the started VM and not to something else on the local network.

Okay, I haven't been following this too closely, so this may be a stupid question: isn't the easiest solution to just add a `touch known_hosts` to `setup()`?? https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/3dace12777e27a7732acb25ae78f86ced8ae04da/pkg/machine/e2e/machine_test.go#L78-L87

IIRC the `known_hosts` must actually list the expected matching key. An empty file does not allow making a connection.

---

Cc: @cdoern to be sure he is in the loop.
Okay, maybe also write `StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new` when writing the config?

The code enforcing the public key entry presence is Go code (added by the linked PRs), it doesn’t interpret all of the OpenSSH options. IIRC right now it just doesn’t have any code to add an entry to `known_hosts`, under any circumstances.

Go expert needed. Looks like go lint is smart enough to realize that `Skip()` means "code below doesn't get run", but too stupid to realize that `Skip()` is temporary and it should just shut up. So:
```
pkg/machine/e2e/config_basic_test.go:15:24: func `(*basicMachine).withPodmanCommand` is unused (unused)
func (s *basicMachine) withPodmanCommand(args []string) *basicMachine {
                       ^
pkg/machine/e2e/config_basic_test.go:7:23: func `basicMachine.buildCmd` is unused (unused)
func (s basicMachine) buildCmd(m *machineTestBuilder) []string {
                      ^
pkg/machine/e2e/config_basic_test.go:3:6: type `basicMachine` is unused (unused)
type basicMachine struct {
     ^
```
Go expert, please give the solution.

Oh, never mind. @lsm5 try this:
```diff
diff --git a/pkg/machine/e2e/basic_test.go b/pkg/machine/e2e/basic_test.go
index 2228259f9..923e7cde4 100644
--- a/pkg/machine/e2e/basic_test.go
+++ b/pkg/machine/e2e/basic_test.go
@@ -1,6 +1,8 @@
 package e2e_test
 
 import (
+       "os"
+
        . "github.com/onsi/ginkgo"
        . "github.com/onsi/gomega"
        . "github.com/onsi/gomega/gexec"
@@ -20,7 +22,9 @@ var _ = Describe("run basic podman commands", func() {
        })
 
        It("Basic ops", func() {
-               Skip("FIXME: #15347 - Basic ops fails on PR runs and on x86_64")
+               if os.Getenv("CIRRUS_CI") != "" {
+                       Skip("FIXME: #15347 - Basic ops fails on PR runs and on x86_64")
+               }
 
                name := randomString()
                i := new(initMachine)
```

Very minimally tested: Just above the `Skip`, do something like
```go
		bmOnlyToMarkUsed := basicMachine{}
		_ = bmOnlyToMarkUsed.withPodmanCommand
		Skip("…")

```

and possibly similarly for other unused methods.

FWIW the `os.Getenv` approach is a better solution, because it continues to make the rest of the test fully visible to the validator, and bugs introduced in that code would be more likely to be caught.

I’d only recommend a minor improvement: something like `if os.Getenv("this-variable-is-never-expected-to-be-set") != "and-especially-not-to-this-value"`, to make the test behave consistently in CI and local runs.
… and to add a comment to the `Getenv` call explaining that the purpose is to make the `Skip` invisible to dead-code linters.
PTAL at changes.

Sorry that I missed this today. If needed I can assist @baude with the known_hosts patch. Everything @mtrmac is right, we opted not to write to a file if the key did not exist. 

Adding a way to enable/disable this (with the default option to be disable) seems like the best fix. I can fire away a c/common PR if no one has tried that route yet. 

I'm back from PTO this Sunday, so I will look for other issues with the machine tests that might have slipped through before then to ensure nothing else breaks.

> I’d only recommend a minor improvement: something like if os.Getenv("this-variable-is-never-expected-to-be-set") != "and-especially-not-to-this-value", to make the test behave consistently in CI and local runs.

Careful, for anybody using `hack/get_ci_vm.sh` the `$CIRRUS_CI` variable won't be set, but IIRC `$GET_CI_VM` is set non-zero.

That's a feature (albeit not one I had intended): anyone using `get_ci_vm` is going to be doing so expressly for the purpose of fixing this bug, so they don't want the test skipped.

The important thing right now is to get this PR merged, and the way to do that is by skipping the test in Cirrus.

> is going to be doing so expressly for the purpose of fixing this bug

Well, we can hope at least.  Otherwise this will be a constant nag for anybody trying to play with anything podman-machine related in `get_ci_vm`.  Many times, temporary==permanent, which is why I'm nervous.

Either way, you're right, we do need to get this PR merged ASAP.
@baude @edsantiago @cevich good to go. PTAL.
@lsm5 I'm sitting here watching the spinning CI and waiting for it to finish...

> @lsm5 I'm sitting here watching the spinning CI and waiting for it to finish...

yup :laughing: I meant apart from the artifacts test

/lgtm

\o/
Good job everybody, esp. @lsm5 leading this.  Pretty soon I'll be completely out of a job :rofl:

> Good job everybody, esp. @lsm5 leading this. Pretty soon I'll be completely out of a job rofl

with my awesome yaml skills, you have at least a 100 years worth of job security :laughing:
